title: Only 3 People on Earth Have This Brand-New Blood Type!
summary: Imagine having blood so unusual that almost nobody else on the planet shares it. That's what happened in a Thai hospital, where doctors discovered a new blood type called B(A). Only three people are known to have it—one patient and two donors. Blood types are like secret codes on your red blood cells. They're made of tiny markers called antigens (say: AN-tih-jenz), which are sugars and proteins. Your immune system uses them to tell what belongs in your body and what doesn't. Most of us know the main types: A, B, AB, and O. But there are actually 48 different blood types in humans—and now B(A) makes 49! This new type mostly looks like B, but it also has a sneaky little bit of A antigen. Scientists found that all three people with B(A) had the same unusual change in their genes. It's the first time this type has been seen in Thai people, though similar oddballs have popped up in other parts of Asia. Why does this matter? When someone needs a blood transfusion (getting blood from another person), the antigens must match. If they don't, the body might attack the new blood. That's why finding rare types like B(A) helps doctors keep patients safe. Who knows—maybe you're the fourth person with this super-rare blood!
why_it_matters: Rare blood types matter because one day you or someone you love might need a blood transfusion, and scientists work to make sure there's a match for everyone.

keywords.explanation: Tiny markers on cells that help your body tell what belongs inside you and what does not.
The body's defense team that fights off germs and attacks things that don't belong.
When doctors give blood from one person to another who needs it.
Instructions inside your cells that decide your traits, like hair color or blood type.
People who give something—like blood—to help others.

What are the tiny markers on red blood cells called?
How many total blood types are there now?
The new blood type B(A) mostly looks like which type?
What must match when someone gets blood from another person?
What did scientists find was the same in all three people with B(A)?
options: An unusual change in their genes
questions.correct_answer: An unusual change in their genes

background_read: Blood types were first discovered in 1901 by scientist Karl Landsteiner.
Today we know blood types are determined by which antigens you inherit from your parents.
Some blood types are very rare and found mostly in certain parts of the world.
Article_Structure: WHO: Doctors in a Thai hospital and three rare-blooded people
WHAT: A brand-new blood type called B(A)
WHY: Because matching antigens during blood transfusions keeps patients safe

perspectives.description: Doctors are excited because knowing about this type can prevent dangerous transfusion reactions.
With only three known people, if one of them needs blood, it's very hard to find a match.
Scientists are fascinated by how genes can create such unusual blood types.
